coredata.dc:title: Optimization of perovskite/carbon interface performance using N-doped coal-based graphene quantum dots and its mechanism analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qixu
Yang, Xiaoyu
Qi, Ying
Wei, Peng
Cheng, Jian
Xie, Yahong
coredata.dc:description: Optimizing the interfacial properties between perovskite and carbon electrodes has always been an important way to improve the photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) of carbon-based perovskite solar cells (C-PSCs) and facilitate their commercialization. In this paper, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with fluorescent properties were successfully prepared using inexpensive coal as raw material by a facile and environmentally friendly chemical reagent oxidation. The results show that the electron-rich pyridinic nitrogen in N-GQDs can act as Lewis bases to form coordination bonds with uncoordinated lead ions by sharing electron pairs, thereby reducing the defect density and nonradiative recombination of photo-generated electron-hole, and extending lifetime of charge carriers. In addition, due to the passivation of N-GQDs, the hysteresis effect of the device is significantly reduced and the long-term stability is also improved. By optimizing the concentration, the PCE of C-PSCs achieved a maximum of 14.31%, which was improved by 20.25% compared with 11.90% of the pristine C-PSCs. This work provides a facile, environmentally friendly and efficient strategy for improving the overall performance of C-PSCs using inexpensive coal-based N-GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-based PSCs
Nitrogen-doped GQDs
Reducing the defect density
Nonradiative recombination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The preparation process of N-GQDs. (b) TEM image of N-GQDs. Inset: the histogram of lateral size distribution. (c) HRTEM image of N-GQDs. (d) UV-Vis and PL spectra of N-GQDs. (e) XPS survey spectrum of N-GQDs. The corresponding high resolution XPS spectra of (f) C 1s, (g) O 1s and (h) N 1s.

Characterization of the MAPbI3 and MAPbI3/N-GQDs perovskite films: (a) XPS survey spectrum of the two kinds of perovskite films. The corresponding high resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) Pb 4f, (d) N 1s, (f) I 3d. (e) The corresponding high resolution XPS spectra of N 1s from N-GQDs and MAPbI3/N-GQDs perovskite film.

Top-view SEM images of the (a) MAPbI3 and (b) MAPbI3/N-GQDs perovskite films. Two-dimensional AFM images and corresponding three-dimensional plots of the (c, e) MAPbI3 and (d, f) MAPbI3/N-GQDs perovskite films. (g) Height variation along the red line marked in part (c). (h) Height variation along the blue line marked in part (d).

Characterization of the MAPbI3 and MAPbI3/N-GQDs perovskite films: (a) XRD patterns, (b) UV-Vis spectroscopy and (c) PL emission spectra of the two kinds of perovskite films. (d) I-V curves for perovskite films with and without N-GQDs passivation based on FTO/MAPbI3/Carbon structure. (e) Dark J-V curves from hole-only devices based on the two kinds of perovskite films. (f) Trap density and carrier mobility for the two kinds of perovskite films from SCLC measurement.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of C-PSC with a structure of FTO/SnO2/ MAPbI3/N-GQDs/Carbon.

Characterization of the pristine and N-GQDs passivated devices: (a, b) PCE, J sc, V oc, FF box-and-whisker plots of the two kinds of devices. (c) J-V curves and (d) steady-state output curves of the current density and efficiency for the two kinds of devices. (e) The slope of the normalized J-V curves at each voltage. (f) Plots of –dV/dJ vs. (J sc–J)−1 and linear fitting for the two kinds of devices.e.

Characterization of the pristine and N-GQDs passivated devices. (a) Dark J-V curves of the two kinds of devices. (b) J-V curves scanned both forward and reverse directions. (c) Mott-Schottky plots of the two kinds of devices. (d) Nyquist plots and (e) Bode curves of the two kinds of devices measured at a reverse potential of 0.8 V. Inset: the equivalent circuit model and local enlargement. (f) Open-circuit voltage decay and Electron lifetime curves. (g) J-V curves under dark condition. (h) Plots of J ph with respect to the V eff. (i) Normalized photocurrent (J ph/J sat) with respect to V eff.
Schematic illustration of the passivation mechanism of N-GQDs for the MAPbI3 perovskite.
Long-term stability of two types of unpackaged C-PSCs tested at room temperature.
Summary of the performance photovoltaic parameters of the two kinds of devices with various concentrations of N-GQDs passivation.

head.ce:title: Optimization of perovskite/carbon interface performance using N-doped coal-based graphene quantum dots and its mechanism analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources; School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources; School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Xinjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources; School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials & Chemical Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials & Chemical Engineering, Hanyang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials & Chemical Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-GQDs were successfully prepared from coal by a facile and environment-friendly oxidation, and successfully passivated Pb2+ defects. Compared with the pristine cells, the photoelectric conversion efficiency is increased by 20.25%.

Optimizing the interfacial properties between perovskite and carbon electrodes has always been an important way to improve the photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) of carbon-based perovskite solar cells (C-PSCs) and facilitate their commercialization. In this paper, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with fluorescent properties were successfully prepared using inexpensive coal as raw material by a facile and environmentally friendly chemical reagent oxidation. The results show that the electron-rich pyridinic nitrogen in N-GQDs can act as Lewis bases to form coordination bonds with uncoordinated lead ions by sharing electron pairs, thereby reducing the defect density and nonradiative recombination of photo-generated electron-hole, and extending lifetime of charge carriers. In addition, due to the passivation of N-GQDs, the hysteresis effect of the device is significantly reduced and the long-term stability is also improved. By optimizing the concentration, the PCE of C-PSCs achieved a maximum of 14.31%, which was improved by 20.25% compared with 11.90% of the pristine C-PSCs. This work provides a facile, environmentally friendly and efficient strategy for improving the overall performance of C-PSCs using inexpensive coal-based N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based perovskite solar cells (C-PSCs) have shown great potential in photovoltaic devices and large-scale production due to their low cost, facile fabrication process and excellent environmental stability [1,2]. However, certain critical issues such as inferior energy matching and poor interface contact between perovskite film and carbon electrode hinder the further improvement of their photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) [3,4].

At present, the preparation of perovskite films mainly adopts the method of low-temperature solution-processing and fast crystallization, which has the advantages of simplicity and low-cost. However, ionic defects inevitably produce at grain boundaries and film surfaces [5–7], such as positively charged uncoordinated Pb ions and halide vacancies, negatively charged Pb–I anti-site defects or uncoordinated halide ions [8,9]. These ionic defects may be the centers for the nonradiative recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes, reducing the number of carriers and shortening the carrier lifetime which are the major obstacles to achieve high PCEs and long-term stability of C-PSCs.

As a new member of nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) not only have high conductivity, but also have the advantages of good chemical stability, excellent solubility, and cost-effective. In addition, GQDs usually possess various functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl, which makes them shown promising applications in PSCs. Bian et al. deposited N-GQDs on the top of PSCs, which effectively converted harmful UV light into useful visible photons. The optimal device exhibited an increased J sc of 19.15 mA cm−2 from 18.67 mA cm−2 and a PCE of 16.02% from 15.53% [10]. Xie et al. introduced GQDs into the electron transport layer (ETL), which improved the electron extraction efficiency in the ETL and reduced the recombination at the ETL/perovskite interface, thereby increasing the PCE and reducing the hysteresis effect [11]. Gan et al. incorporated N-GQDs with abundant N active sites into perovskite precursor solution and effectively passivated the grain boundary defects through Lewis base/acid interaction [12]. Guo et al. deposited p-type GQDs at PEDOT:PSS/perovskite interface, which regulated the energy level and the charge distribution [13]. The above researches indicate that appropriate incorporation of GQDs into PSCs can effectively improve the performance of the devices. However, the synthesis process of N-GQDs is relatively complicated and the research on its passivation mechanism on perovskite materials is still not sufficient.

In this work, ultralow-cost coal with abundant pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, which can strongly interact with lead ions in the perovskite materials, was used as carbon source to in-situ prepare N-GQDs using facile and environmentally friendly chemical reagent oxidation. The effect of passivation of N-GQDs on the photovoltaic properties of C-PSCs was studies and the passivation mechanism was investigated. In view of the advantages of low price of coal-based N-GQDs, our work provides a simple and facile approach to improve the optoelectronic performance and stability of C-PSCs.

Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conductive glass was purchased from Ying Kou OPV Tech New Energy Co., Ltd. Methanaminium iodide (MAI) (99.9%) and lead iodide (PbI2) (≥99.5%) were purchased from Xi’an Polymer Light Technology Corp. SnCl2·2H2O (99.99%), ethyl acetate (99.5%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (99.9%), chlorobenzene (99%) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (99.8%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Carbon pastes were purchased from Japan JUJO chemical Co., Ltd. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

In this research, the coal from Dahuangshan in Xinjiang, China was selected as the raw material for the manufacture of N-GQD through chemical reagent oxidation. First, the pulverized coal was pre-ground into fine powders with a diameter of less than 74 μm, and then carbonized in a tube furnace at 600 °C for 1 h under Ar flow. The obtained carbon powders were mixed with a certain amount of deionized water and put into a 100 mL ball grinding tank, fixed on a KMT-1 planetary ball mill, ground at 600 r min−1 for 3 h at room temperature. After drying, 1.0 g of the obtained ultrafine pulverized coal was added into 100 mL of 10% H2O2, stirred and refluxed at 80 °C for 10 h. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation and dialyzed for two days. The resulting solution was freeze-dried to obtain coal-based N-GQDs powder for use.

First, FTO glasses were continuously ultrasonically cleaned in deionized water, isopropanol, and ethanol for 20 min, then treated with UV/ozone for 15 min. The SnO2 electron transport layer (ETL) was prepared by spin-coating SnO2 precursor solution at 3000 r min−1 for 30 s onto the FTO substrate and annealing at 200 °C for 60 min. MAPbI3 precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 159 mg MAI and 461 mg PbI2 (molar ratio = 1:1) in 0.7 mL anhydrous DMF and DMSO (the volume ratio of DMF to DMSO was 9:1). After fully stirring to dissolve, 60 μL of this perovskite precursor solution was spin-coated on the SnO2 ETL, and at the 13th second, 500 μL of ethyl acetate was continuously added dropwise as an anti-solvent and annealed at 100 °C for 10 min to obtain a perovskite layer. The as-prepared N-GQDs powders were dispersed in IPA and sonicated for 1 h to obtain a uniformly dispersed supernatant, which was then filtered twice with a 0.2 μm teflon filter, and spin-coated onto the MAPbI3 perovskite film at 3000 r min−1 for 30 s followed by thermal annealing at 80 °C for 10 min. The pristine perovskite film was denoted as MAPbI3, and the perovskite film passivated with N-GQDs was denoted as MAPbI3/N-GQDs. Commercially available carbon paste was used in the experiment. 3 g of commercial carbon paste, 2 mL of chlorobenzene and 1.5 g of zirconia beads together into a glass bottle and grinded using an oscillator for 1 h. The carbon electrode was fabricated on the perovskite layer via the doctor blade method, and the thickness was controlled by two layers of polyimide tape (120 μm). The above operation was repeated, and the obtained device was dried at 60 °C for 10 min to finish the device fabrication. The above operations were all carried out under atmospheric conditions with a humidity of 20%–30%. The C-PSC using MAPbI3/N-GQDs as light absorber was denoted as C-PSC/N-GQDs.

The optical properties of the coal-based N-GQDs, MAPbI3 and the MAPbI3/N-GQDs films were tested by an UV–visible spectrometer (UV 3600, Shimadzu, Japan) as well as a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Japan). The microstructure of the N-GQDs was characterized by a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Talos F200S). The morphologies of the pristine MAPbI3 and the MAPbI3/N-GQDs films were observed by field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU8010, Hitachi) as well as atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker, Dimension Fast Scan and Dimension Icon). The crystal structures were obtained by X-ray diffraction (XRD, PHILIPSPW1800 diffractometer with Cu-K α radiation). The J-V curves of PSCs were measured using a CHI660D electrochemical workstation at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1 under irradiation of simulated solar light intensity controlled at AM 1.5 G (100 mW cm−2). Light intensity was calibrated by a standard silicon solar cell. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out by CHI660D electrochemical workstation from 0.1 MHz–0.1 Hz frequency at a bias voltage of 0.8 V in the dark and its Nyquist curve was fitted by the Z-view software. Trap density and carrier mobility of the pristine MAPbI3 and the MAPbI3/N-GQDs films using hole-only devices with a structure of FTO/NiO/MAPbI3/Carbon electrode were evaluated in the dark by the space-charge-limited current (SCLC) measurement. Among them, NiO was prepared and spin-coated following the literature [14].

The product obtained according to the preparation process of Fig. 1 (a) was dispersed into ethanol and its morphology was characterized by TEM. As can be seen from the TEM images shown in Fig. 1(b) that the obtained N-GQDs powders are well dispersed without obvious agglomeration, and their aqueous solution can still maintain a stable homogeneous phase at room temperature even after 1 month. Moreover, the particle size of N-GQDs was mainly distributed in 2–4 nm, and an average size of 3.55 nm. Clear crystalline lattice fringes with a characteristic spacing of 0.24 nm also can be observed from the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 1(c), which can be attributed to the (110) diffraction planes of graphene [10], preliminarily confirming the formation of GQDs. From the UV–Vis absorption and steady-state PL spectrum (Fig. 1d), it can be observed that the resulting carbon dots have no light absorption in the visible region, but have a strong and broad steady-state fluorescence emission peak centered at 451 nm in the 365–590 nm wavelength range. Correspondingly, a strong cyan emission can be observed under ultraviolet light (λ = 375 nm) (Fig. 1a), which is a typical feature of GQDs, further confirming the successful synthesis of GQDs.

Combined with the XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 1(e), it is clearly found that there are not only C, O elements, but also N element in the prepared GQDs. More details can be seen from the high-resolution XPS patterns of C1s and the corresponding deconvolution results shown in Fig. 1(f), which shows four major peaks at 284.7, 285.3, 286.2 and 288.6 eV, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C–N, C–O and C=O, respectively [15,16]. Two peaks seen from the O 1s spectrum in Fig. 1(g) can correspond to C=O (531.7 eV) and C–O–C/C–OH (533.1 eV), respectively [17]. Notably, the N 1s spectra exhibits three peaks in Fig. 1(h), confirming that the introduced nitrogen can be classified into three types of nitrogen functional groups, namely pyridinic nitrogen (398.7 eV), pyrrolic nitrogen (399.6 eV) and graphitic nitrogen (401.0 eV) [15]. From the intensity of XPS spectra, it can be concluded that the doped nitrogen is dominated by pyrrolic nitrogen and pyridinic nitrogen, and the content of graphitic nitrogen is the lowest.

XPS characterization was also performed for the pristine film and the perovskite film treated with 1 mg mL−1 N-GQDs to understand the passivation mechanism and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2(a), the treatment of N-GQDs did not change the elemental composition of the perovskite films. In the high-resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. 2b), the C–C and C–N bonds can be attributed to the presence of MA+ cations, while the unexpected C=O bond located at 288.6 eV reflects the decomposition of perovskite, which maybe the result of interaction with air during preparation. The peak area ratio of C=O shows a significant decline after treatment of N-GQDs, indicating that treatment of N-GQDs can slow down the damage of the perovskite layer from oxygen and moisture in the environment and improve the air stability of the device [18]. Fig. 2(c) is the high-resolution spectra of Pb 4f, from which two main peaks of Pb 4f 5/2 (143.1 eV) and Pb 4f 7/2 (138.2 eV) can be observed, which should be related to the saturated coordination of Pb2+. After passivation by N-GQDs, these two main peaks decreased from 143.1 and 138.2 eV to 142.72 and 137.8 eV, illustrating that electron cloud density around Pb2+ increased. This implies that there is an interaction between the N-GQDs and undercoordinated Pb2+ in the perovskite and donates electron density for the under-coordinated Pb2+ with net positive charges, resulting in a decrease of binding energies of the Pb 4f peak. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s for MAPbI3 film has only one peak centered at 402.13 eV, which can be ascribed to the N-methyl C bond in MA+ [19]. In contrast, the N 1s spectrum for MAPbI3/N-GQDs film exhibits three new peaks, confirming the adsorption of N-GQDs on the perovskite films, thus providing an opportunities for passivation. In the next discussion section, we will focus on analyzing the mechanism of these three types of nitrogen functional groups participating in the passivation.

As shown in Fig. 2(e), after passivation by N-GQDs, the peaks of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N shift to the higher binding energy, suggesting a decrease in the electron cloud density around the N atoms, which is attributed to the electron-donor effect of the N atoms to Pb2+. Pyridinic nitrogen has a lone electron pair, which forms the coordination bonds by sharing the lone electron pair with the empty 6p orbit of Pb2+, thereby reducing defect density and nonradiative recombination in the perovskite bulk. Furthermore, for the MAPbI3 perovskite film, the two weak peaks at 141.3 and 136.3 eV can be attributed to metallic Pb (Pb0) [20], which can not only act as a recombination center to reduce the photovoltaic performance of PSCs [21], but also accelerate degradation of perovskite, and thus is considered to be one of the factors contributing to the reduction of both efficiency and stability for PSCs. In general, for high-performance PSCs, the generation of Pb0 during the preparation of perovskite films should be avoided. Surprisingly, the peaks of Pb0 disappear after the treatment of N-GQDs, implying that the recombination centers were reduced, and thus less nonradiative recombination. The negligible shift of the peak of graphitic N indicates that there is no charge transfer between the graphitic N and the undercoordinated Pb2+. Therefore, we speculate that pyridinic N and pyrrolic N have stronger electron donating/sharing ability like Lewis base, and they are more likely to interact with uncoordinated ions through coordination bonds. On the surface of perovskite film, dangling bonds can act as nonradiative recombination centers to degrade device performance. As shown in Fig. 2(f), after treatment with N-GQDs, the I 3d spectrum shifts toward the lower binding energy direction demonstrating that the coordination of the Pb–I bond changes and the bond strength decreases [12]. This can be attributed to the coordination of the electron-rich pyrrolic nitrogen with the dangling bond of Pb through electron donation, resulting in the weakening of the Pb–I bonds and reduction of nonradiative centers [22].

It is known that the surface morphology and the grain size of the perovskite play crucial roles in determining the performance of PSCs. To have better insight the effect of post-treatment of N-GQDs on perovskite films, SEM and AFM characterizations were performed on the pristine MAPbI3 and the MAPbI3/N-GQDs films. Fig. 3 (a and b) exhibit the top-view SEM images of the perovskite films before and after post-treatment of N-GQDs, from which, it can be observed that the surfaces of two kinds of films are irregular block with an average grain size of 200–300 nm, and no obvious pinholes are found, demonstrating a good compactness. After passivation treatment with N-GQDs, the surface morphology of the MAPbI3 perovskite film can still be seen from the SEM image, indicating that passivation treatment didn’t form an isolation layer. Fig. 3(c–f) show the two-dimensional AFM images and the corresponding three-dimensional maps of the two kinds of perovskite films. In figures, the Ra and RMS values decreased with the passivation of N-GQDs, indicating that the films turned to be flatter after passivation of N-GQDs, which is considered to be beneficial to improve the coverage of subsequent carbon electrodes. As illustrated in Fig. 3(g and h), the passivation of N-GQDs reduces the height difference between the grain surface and the grain boundary of the perovskite film, which is presumably due to the non-volatile N-GQDs partially deposited at the grain boundary after heating [6].

Next, the influence of the post-treatment of N-GQDs on the crystallinity of perovskite was investigated by XRD patterns. In Fig. 4 (a), it can be seen that the two kinds of perovskite films have similar diffraction peaks and there is no additional diffraction peaks, butthe diffraction intensity of the MAPbI3/N-GQDs film is higher, suggesting that the post-treatment of N-GQDs did not change the crystal structure of perovskite films, but increased its crystallinity. Among them, the four sharp peaks located at 14.08°, 19.97°, 28.38°, and 32.85° can be indexed as the (110), (112), (220), and (310) planes of the perovskite structure, respectively, and no peaks of PbI2 is observed, suggesting that the PbI2 was completely converted into perovskite crystals [19,23].

The effect of post-treatment of N-GQDs on the optical properties of perovskite films was investigated by UV–Vis spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(b). By comparing, it is found that the two kinds of perovskite films exhibit similar absorption profiles and coincident absorption edges, but the absorption intensity of the MAPbI3/N-GQDs film increased slightly in the wavelength range from 550 to 800 nm, indicating that the thickness of the perovskite films barely changed, and N-GQDs passivation has no effect on perovskite band gap [24].

Fig. 4(c) shows the PL emission spectra of two types of perovskite films which were coated on cleaned glass substrates to avoid the influence of charge injection between the active layer and the electron transfer materials. PL intensity is related to the number of nonradiative recombination centers in the perovskite film [14,23]. In general, the deep-level energy defects are the main reason for non-radiative recombination [25]. The MAPbI3/N-GQDs film shows much higher PL intensity, which combined with XPS analysis can be attributed to the reduction of undercoordinated Pb2+ and Pb0 defects, which is beneficial to the promotion of carrier transport and prolongs the carrier lifetime [7]. At the same time, the PL peak position of MAPbI3/N-GQDs film show slight red shift, which may be due to the passivation treatment enhanced crystallinity or reduced defects of the perovskite film [26].

It is generally believed that a reduction in defect density also increases conductivity. The conductivity change of the perovskite films is indirectly illustrated by current–voltage (I-V) curves of the architecture of FTO/MAPbI3/Carbon with and without N-GQDs passivation. The direct current conductivities of both films are investigated in Fig. 5 (d), which was calculated by the Eq. (1).
(1)
σ = D / ( S R )
where D is the thickness of perovskite films, S is the test area and R is the corresponding resistance. Since the thickness and test area of the two kinds of films are the same, σ depends entirely on R, which is the slope of the I-V curve. In contrast, the linear I-V curves of the C-PSC/N-GQDs shows a larger slope, which means higher conductivity and better carrier transport property.

The dark J-V curves from hole-only devices shown in Fig. 4(e) are divided into three regions: Ohmic region, Trap-filled limit (TFL) region, and Child region. In the Ohmic region, the current density is proportional to the voltage, and can reflect the ohmic contact resistance. The C-PSC/N-GQDs shows a larger current density, indicating that the passivation reduces the ohmic resistance and improves the conductivity of the films, which is consistent with the dark I-V curve results (Fig. 4d). In the TFL region, the current density increases nonlinearly with the voltage, and all available trap are completely filled with injected carriers. The kink point between the Ohmic region and the TFL region is defined as the trap filled limit voltage (V TFL), and the trap states density (N trap) can be calculated using the following Eq. (2) [27].
(2)
N
t r a p
=
( 2 ε
ε 0
V
T F L
)
( q
L
2
)
where q is the elementary charge (1.6 × 1019C), L is the thickness of the perovskite film, ε 0 is the vacuum permittivity, and ε is the relative dielectric constant of MAPbI3. The results show that the value of V TFL decreased from 0.95 to 0.69 V, and the corresponding calculated value of N trap decreased from 3.20 × 1016 to 2.32 × 1016 cm−3 after modification with N-GQDs, implying that the post-treatment of N-GQDs could reduce charge defects, and combined with XPS analysis, they are mainly Pb2+ defects [24]. In the Child region, the current density is proportional to the square of the voltage, and the carrier mobility (μ) can be calculated from the Mott-Gurney law in the single-carrier devices using Eq. (3) [27].
(3)
μ =
( 8
L
3
J D
)
/
( 9 ε
ε 0
V
2
)
where L, J D, and V are the perovskite film thickness, current density and bias voltage, respectively. The carrier mobilities of the pristine and C-PSC/N-GQDs are 1.51 × 10−2 and 1.72 × 10−2 cm2 V−1 s−1, respectively, which may be one of the main reason for the improved conductivity of perovskite films. The SCLC parameters are summarized in Fig. 4(f), where the lower defect density and higher carrier mobility indicate the improved performance of the film.

Schematic diagram of the preparation of C-PSC with a structure of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3/N-GQDs/Carbon is shown in Fig. 5. In order to obtain the best performance, the concentrations of N-GQDs was optimized, the corresponding photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1 .

As can be seen from Fig. 6 (a and b), PSC treated with 1 mg mL−1 N-GQDs shows the best photovoltaic performance and the narrowest PCE distribution, demonstrating good reproducibility. When the concentration of N-GQDs reaches 2 mg mL−1, the photovoltaic performance of cell starts to decline. We speculate that excessive N-GQDs may be distributed non-uniformity and aggregate at grain boundaries, which would result in bad contact between carbon electrode and perovskite layer. The large aggregation of N-GQDs may also become the recombination centers and increase nonradiative recombination. From the J-V curves shown in Fig. 6(c), the champion device optimized by 1 mg mL−1 N-GQDs gives a V oc of 0.949 V, a J sc of 23.25 mA cm−2, and a FF of 0.65, which is obvious higher than that of the pristine one. Finally, the resulting maximum PCE reached 14.31% (average 13.63%), which is 20.25% higher than 11.90% (average 11.15%) of the pristine devices, under standard AM 1.5G continuous illumination for 200 s, the steady-state output curves of the C-PSC and C-PSC/N-GQDs at the maximum power output point were obtained as shown in Fig. 6(d), which is consistent with the J-V analysis, confirming the validity of PCE and J sc.

The light J-V curves (Fig. 6c) were normalized and the slope as a function of applied voltage was extracted and shown in Fig. 6(e). Ideally, when the circuit is in open circuit conditions (OC), a near-vertical steep slope represents a rapid transition from MPP to OC, indicating efficient current collection [5]. As shown in Fig. 6(e), steeper curves near V oc were observed for the C-PSC/N-GQDs, indicating a more efficient current collection capability. According to earlier literature, a planar structured PSC can be treated as a single junction diode, and its J-V characteristic with a large R rec can be described by following Eq. (4) [28].
(4)
J =
J
s c
-
J 0
{ e x p
[ e
( V + J
R s
)
/
( A
K B
T )
]
- 1 }
where J is the current flow through the external load, J sc is the photogenerated current density, J 0 is the dark saturate current density, V is the applied voltage, R s is the series resistance, A is the ideality factor, K B is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature, and e is the electron charge. Based on Eq. (4), the J-V curves can also be described as
- d V / d J =
A
K B
T / e
(
J
s c
- J )
- 1
+
R s
[28].

Fig. 6(f) gives the plots of –dV/dJ vs. (J sc–J)−1 and linear fitting, from which it can be seen that there is a good linear relationship between –dV/dJ and (J sc–J)−1 in light, which means that the fabricated PSCs are well-behaved heterojunction solar cells to a certain extent. The R s derived from the intercepts of linear fitting of plots was reduced from 3.60 Ω (C-PSC) to 3.19 Ω (C-PSC/N-GQDs), suggesting a lower resistance in charge transfer process.

Based on Eq. (4), the V oc obtained when the current flowing through the external circuit is zero (J = 0) can be described as
V
o c
=
( A
K B
T / e )
l n
(
J
s c
/
J 0
+ 1 )
[28]. It can be seen that a higher V oc corresponds to a lower J 0, which can be evaluated from the current density intercept at zero voltage of the linear regression of the dark J-V plots in the high voltage region (Fig. 7 a) [14]. Fig. 7(a) shows that after passivation with N-GQDs, the value of J0 was reduced by an order of magnitude from 1.08 × 10−8 to 1.05 × 10−9 mA cm−2, which means a higher V oc. Furthermore, from the low voltage region, the leakage current of C-PSC/N-GQDs is one order of magnitude lower than that of the pristine device, indicating that passivation of N-GQDs can effectively suppress leakage current, so that more photocurrent will pass through the device, and leading to higher J sc [29].

In Fig. 7(b), the hysteresis effect of the two PSCs is compared through the J-V curves scanned both forward and reverse directions. After passivation with N-GQDs, the hysteresis effect of the device is significantly reduced, which can be attributed to the reduced defect density and suppressed recombination of charge carriers in perovskite films [6,23].

The values of the built-in potential (V bi) for the pristine and C-PSC/N-GQDs obtained from the intercept on the bias axis of the linear fitting line of the Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. 7c) are 0.96 and 1.04 V, respectively. A larger V bi increases the driving force for dissociating photogenerated carriers and stimulates charge separation, which increases charge extraction at the anode [18,30]. The increase of V bi also increases the V oc of the devices, so N-GQDs passivated devices can obtain higher V oc, which is very good consistent with the higher V oc in J-V curves. The result is further analyzed by fitting the curves according to the following Eq. (5).
(5)
C
- 2
= 2
V -
V
b i
-
(
K B
T / q )
/
( q ε
ε 0
N )
where C represents the space charge capacitance of per unit, and q, ε, and ε 0 are the elementary electron, the dielectric constant of the semiconductor, and the vacuum permittivity, respectively. N, V, V bi, K B, and T are interfacial charge density, applied voltage, build-in potential, Boltzmann constant, and absolute temperature, respectively. In the M-S equation, the N is inversely proportional to the line slope, the larger value of the slope indicates that devices possess a smaller N [29]. Therefore, the C-PSC/N-GQDs show lower charge density at the interface, indicating that the passivated device has excellent charge extraction ability at the interface.

Furthermore, EIS was used to further study the charge transport process and recombination kinetics in PSCs, the Nyquist plots of the pristine and C-PSC/N-GQDs are presented in Fig. 7(d), and the inset shows the equivalent circuit model. Both samples show two semicircles in Fig. 7(d), where R s represents the series resistance of the whole device, corresponding to the intercept on the real axis, and R rec presents the charge-recombination resistance, corresponding to the diameters of the semicircles [31]. Compared with the pristine device, the C-PSC/N-GQDs has a smaller R s value, which can be attributed to the smoother surface and better contact with the carbon electrode of the MAPbI3/N-GQDs film. The larger R rec value indicates that the passivation of N-GQDs can suppress charge recombination process between the perovskite film and the carbon electrode interface, thereby reducing the resistance in charge transfer and significantly improving charge extraction ability [30]. Therefore, the improved R s and R rec for the C-PSC/N-GQDs devices further confirm that perovskite/carbon electrode interface charge recombination was suppressed, and achieved more efficient charge extraction. This is mainly due to the fact that the surface of perovskite films turned to be flatter, increase the surface contact with the carbon electrode. Fig. 7(e) is the Bode curves, and the charge lifetime (τ) can be calculated by the following Eq. (6) [32].
(6)
τ = 1 / ( 2 π
f
m a x
)
where fmax represent the peak frequency. The results show that C-PSC/N-GQDs have a longer charge lifetime, suggesting the efficient inhibition of charge recombination, which is consistent with the results of the Nyquist curve.

The open-circuit photovoltage decay (OCVD) curves (Fig. 7f) show that the V oc of the passivated device has a slower photovoltage decay than the pristine device, which demonstrates a lower charge-carrier-recombination rate, thus more balanced charge-carrier transport. Combined with the information provided by OCVD, the electron lifetime τ r can be calculated by the following Eq. (7) [3].
(7)
τ r
= -
K B
T / e
d
V
o c
/ d t
where K B is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature, e is the electron charge, and dV oc/dt is the derivative of the open-circuit photovoltage transient. The calculated results show that the electron lifetime of C-PSC/N-GQD is longer than that of the pristine device, which indicates that the passivation of N-GQDs reduced the defect density and nonradiative recombination, which is conducive to obtaining higher V oc and J sc output.

To further elucidate the mechanism of the enhanced photovoltaic performance of C-PSC/N-GQDs, J-V characterization was also performed in the dark and the results are shown in Fig. 7(g). Combining the J-V curves under illumination (Fig. 7b) and dark (Fig. 7g), the photocurrent density (J ph) and effective voltage (V eff) can be determined by the following Eqs. (8 and 9) [3].
(8)
J
p h
=
J L
-
J D
(9)
V
e f f
=
V 0
- V
where J L and J D are the current density measured under illumination and in the dark, V 0 is the voltage when J ph = 0, and V is the applied voltage bias, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7(h), in the lower voltage region (V eff < 0.1 V), the J ph in both devices increases sharply with V eff, and in the higher voltage region (V eff > 1 V), the J ph of C-PSC/N-GQDs first reaches a saturation photocurrent value (J sat) indicating that the introducion of N-GQDs enhances the light-harvesting capability of the devices. Fig. 7(i) plots the normalized J ph/J sat of the two devices, which can be used to estimate the charge collection efficiency under operating conditions, a larger J ph/J sat value of the C-PSC/N-GQDs means that more carrier recombination is suppressed, thus increasing the charge collection efficiency [33].

Based on the above analysis, we conclude the synergetic passivation mechanism of the N-GQDs for perovskite films, and given the detailed schematic process for the passivation mechanism in Fig. 8 . According to the Lewis acid-base principle, N-GQDs with electron-rich groups (pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen) can be used as the Lewis base, and Pb2+ with the electron-deficient structure can be used as the Lewis acid. Based on the principle of Lewis acid-base coordination, pyridinic nitrogen has a lone electron pair, and passivate defects by sharing the lone electron pair to form coordinate bonds with the empty 6p orbit of Pb2+. The electron-rich pyrrolic nitrogen by donate electrons to coordinate with Pb dangling bonds. At the same time, Pb0 disappears after the passivation treatment of N-GQDs. The reduction of undercoordinated Pb2+ and Pb0 defects successfully suppress the nonradiative recombination. This is beneficial to the promotion of carrier transport and prolongs the carrier lifetime.

Unencapsulated C-PSCs were stored under ambient conditions (about 30% humidity at room temperature) to test the long-term stability and the changes in normalized photoelectric parameters are shown in Fig. 9 . After 30 days, the pristine C-PSCs and the C-PSC/N-GQDs maintained 72.5% and 80.1% of the initial PCE, respectively, indicating that the passivation of N-GQDs also contributed to the long-term stability of the devices.

In summary, N-GQDs with fluorescent properties were prepared successfully through facile environmentally friendly chemical reagent oxidation using ultralow-cost coal as raw material. XPS analysis revealed that the pyridinic nitrogen in the N-GQDs acts as a Lewis base to form coordination bonds with undercoordinated lead ions by sharing their electron pairs, thereby reducing the hole trap density and effectively passivating Pb2+ defect. Electrochemical analysis results show that the nonradiative recombination of the perovskite film is greatly reduced after passivating by N-GQDs, carrier transport is enhanced and the carrier lifetime is prolonged. As a result, the C-PSC added with 1 mg mL−1 N-GQDs achieved a maximum PCE of 14.31%, which is 20.25% higher than 11.90% of the pristine devices, indicating that the N-GQDs is an effective passivator for improving the optoelectronic properties of C-PSC, and the electron-rich pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen in N-GQDs play a key role as Lewis. Moreover, After 30 days of storage the unencapsulated C-PSC/N-GQDs maintained 80.1% of the initial PCE, suggesting a developed long-term stability. This work provides more theoretical support and mechanism analysis for improving photovoltaic performance of C-PSCs using coal-based N-GQDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52162035), the Open project of State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, the Dalian University of Technology (KF2004), and the Graduate Research Innovation Project of Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (XJ2022G045).
